data.title: Nonresidential Construction Forecast 2025-27: Tapering Down
data.description:
The nonresidential construction sector of the economy has changed substantially in recent years. Contractors in this sector and their suppliers must understand how fabs and data centers have altered the long-time concentrations of the different components of total activity. And public sector contractors should understand the winding down of the 2021 infrastructure bill.

Businesses leaders should expect continued growth of data centers but a retrenchment among semiconductor fabs, with small declines across most other parts of the industry.

Chip Fab Construction

Semiconductor fabs and data centers dominated the growth of nonresidential construction since 2022, as shown in the chart nearby. Chip fabs, though, are tapering off. (The data actually cover all facilities manufacturing computers and electronics, but chip fabs drive the changes.) The surge began with the post-pandemic chip shortage. Automakers had dialed down their chip orders in anticipation of weak sales. When stimulus money pushed up demand for cars, the chip makers had already dedicated their production capacity to other uses. Chip prices soared and new fabs were built as quickly as possible - which is never very quickly. Afterwards, the Biden administration pushed the CHIPS Act, which added federal funding for fabs.

The semiconductor industry, though, has long been to be susceptible to boom-bust cycles. The upfront capital costs are extremely high, while the ongoing production costs are relatively low. Once a fab is built, equipped and the personnel trained, operation continues even if prices drop a good deal. The continued supply into a soft market further depresses prices. Little new capacity is added. Then demand eventually increases, and prices soar because it takes a long time to get new fabs up and running. This cycle is common to many capital-intensive sectors, including mining, airlines and shipping.

Data Center Construction

Although fab construction has peaked, data center construction continues to grow. The earlier wave was driven by cloud storage. Now artificial intelligence needs more data center capability. The first needs for AI were model development: building the AI systems. That continues, but interpretation - using the models to answer questions - requires more data centers. Although the course of AI usage is not easily forecasted, increasing application of the technology is the obvious bet.

Other Private Nonresidential Construction

Some of the other parts of private nonresidential construction were impacted by the pandemic and its aftermath. Warehouse construction rose rapidly as people switched to online shopping, and retail construction dropped. As demand for warehouse space increased, developers saw the opportunity and … overbuilt! It's not a terribly bad sector in most cities, but certainly overbuilt. And retail probably underbuilt. Some of the old shopping malls are now obsolete, but the retail market is enjoying more demand for activities, such as gyms, yoga studios, hands-on pottery shops, etc. Careful development will prove profitable.

Office space was found to be overbuilt when people began working from home in large numbers. Even the brightest markets have above-normal vacancy today, but there are always some unmet needs. Suburban medical offices, for example, are growing.

Hotel/motel demand increased after the pandemic lockdowns were over, leading to rising construction in 2022. Now that sector has adequate capacity, and only small increases will be likely in the coming years. Manufacturing construction not counting chip fabs has declined over the past year and probably has sufficient capacity in most cases.

Public Construction

Public sector construction enjoyed a big boost with the 2021 infrastructure bill, which appropriated $1.2 trillion, more than twice the usual public construction budget. Spending will continue for a couple more years, as many of the projects entail multi-year activity. But the ramp-up phase is over. A little bit more plateau will ensue, and then construction will decline with the end of the huge federal funding. Infrastructure is not nearly as large a priority for Congress as it was a few years back.

Construction Forecast

Total nonresidential construction will taper down. Slower growth in the overall economy dictates lower need for new buildings. Tariffs and limited labor force growth have increased costs, which will prevent marginal ideas from becoming active projects. The sector will not crash but will gradual decline over the next two years.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: Stock Price Action Points To A Market Valuation Weakness
data.description:
The last article covered the anticipated offer that Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (NASDAQ:WBD) would receive for presumably the whole company. This article is going to take that foundation and build on it, beginning with the stock price action and what it means for investors. Most would have expected a stock price backtrack when worries about an eventual offer appeared. But that did not happen. I offer some reasons why (but definitely not all).

Stock Price

Mr. Market often demands proof before assigning the "correct" price for future prospects because Mr. Market has heard all kinds of improvement stories before that did not work out. But that often gives the average investor a solid contrarian opportunity.

As can be seen from the above chart, the stock price was absolutely decimated by all the reorganizations and write-offs, even if any of that had no effect upon future profitability.

As was noted in the last article and several before that, management found the initial acquisition situation much worse than they thought it was before the acquisition closed. Nonetheless, I gave management the credit for "fixing things right." This was my job for many years.

I do not know how many comments and articles assigned an artificial deadline that was thought to be "reasonable." I honestly had some of those bosses, and none survived because usually higher-up people wanted things fixed correctly, and they knew what "correctly" looked like.

But Mr. Market can be impatient and shortsighted. That can lead to stock price decimation even while the profit outlook improves because the situation is not moving at the speed Mr. Market likes. That is before you get to market doubts that the right solution was picked in the first place. There is an old saying on Wall Street about "kissing a toad to get a prince, when the reality is the corporate backyard is loaded with toads."

Potential Offer Effect

However, when there was a rumor about an expected offer, it gave a credibility to what management has done (and is about to do) that the market could not otherwise properly gauge.

The result is shown above in a resilient stock price at a much higher level than one would have expected after the offer did not come through. The contrarian idea began to work out long before I expected, thanks to the rumors of an offer that now appear unlikely.

John Malone's Actions

As I noted in an old article, John Malone (WBD Chair Emeritus) stated that he sold puts to support the board after the acquisition. He thought he could make a good profit from that particular price at the time.

It is very unlikely that John Malone changed his mind simply because the stock went down. This is very different from the market where many thought a good profit (if there was one at all) was 20% above the current price, or if they were really ambitious, 100% above the current price.

But I would bet that many of the large investors represented by the board never gave up on the original goal and were willing to give management whatever time it took to get going in that direction.

A simple example of this is my "house half-finished example." No one buys a house that is halfway done. Every time I had a chance at one of these, there was usually far less competition to buy into such a situation. As a result, the price was usually always more than worth the effort. But when the house was finished (and done correctly), it was no problem selling it or renting it for a good return. Over the years, some of these were in great locations during boom times, and some of the largest real estate companies could not sell them when they were half done or needed major improvements.

So, what was going on with Warner Bros. Discovery common stock was really typical human behavior. Going against that behavior is hard. But as the recent stock price action shows, it can be very rewarding even if it is a contrarian idea.

I still believe that the Warner Bros. Discovery Board is now holding management accountable for the original goals (with some adjustments) because many of them want those profits that they originally saw when the initial acquisition proposal was reviewed. But that could mean a potential triple in value over the next five years to make up for everything management had to fix to get where it is now.

Basis For Those Profits

With quarter 3 earnings coming up in early November, there are several things to focus on that may well reinforce what has already happened. Some of these may eventually do more than support the current stock price.

As the schedule above shows, both streaming and studios are doing better in the current fiscal year from an EBITDA standpoint. Management has a profit goal for each, and previous articles have covered the launch timing of the new strategy. Because both have launched growth strategies within the last year or so, Mr. Market was not giving either any credit for the future looking better than all the past "house cleaning moves" that hid the improvements underway.

Now those profit goals announced at all the road shows and quarterly releases gain credibility because someone was interested in buying the company for its future profitability, not its history of losses.

Studios, in particular, have been scoring one hit after another instead of the mediocre (at best) results that marked previous years (or at least inconsistency). While there has been a lot of focus on the streaming EBITDA improvement, studios have been a big surprise to the upside in the current fiscal year.

Debt

Debt retirement outperformance has been a given ever since the acquisition from AT&T (T). That is likely to continue into the future.

With this schedule, the market could have some free cash flow disappointments because there were a lot of nonrecurring items (covered in past articles) that led to much more free cash flow than originally anticipated. While this enabled debt repayment overperformance, it also means the conversion to operational free cash flow could be bumpy going forward.

The key bright spot is how low that debt ratio has gone in such a short period of time. For a leveraged buyout to be successful, often the early deleveraging is the key. Here, the deleveraging outperformance is outstanding. Now that management is executing its growth strategy, the deleveraging can be expected to continue, even if at a slower pace.

Summary

Because the market demanded proof that management knew what it was doing and there would be benefits from what it did, the stock price went to unusually low bargain levels.

But that makes Warner Bros. Discovery a continuing Strong Buy. The rumored deal likely did not happen because the board represents major stockholders that were not going cheaply. No guarantees, of course. However, the whole situation highlighted the original profit goals likely for investors of this stature and that those goals would not be changing because the stock price went down.

No matter what happens, this company is likely to be worth a whole lot more in the future with or without a deal to sell the company (or split it in two).

Now let us see what happens.

Risks

Just because John Malone and other big investors think they can make (a lot of) money does not mean they will achieve their goals. Someone like John Malone is right more often than wrong. But this idea is only one decision, and it can fail to meet expectations.

Management may have the wrong growth plans in one or more areas going forward. This could set back future profitability goals. It could also mean that Mr. Market was right to be pessimistic.

The continuing progress of movies and streaming is far from assured. A bumpy ride from one or both is more likely.

data.title: VIS: Industrials Reach Record, Despite Lagging The S&P 500 (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
“Sell Rosh Hashanah, Buy Yom Kippur.” That’s old Wall Street folklore. Stocks indeed stumbled initially last week, but the bulls quickly dusted themselves off and finished Q3 with a flourish. The Vanguard Industrials Index Fund (NYSEARCA:VIS) settled at a fresh all-time high on Yom Kippur 2025.

I had a "Hold" rating on VIS back in May, noting potential resistance on the chart. That did not play out, and the Industrials sector launched into the end of the first half. Gains were not without speed bumps, however. In fact, VIS’s relative strength petered out in July.

The last two-plus months have been a tougher slog, but with bullish seasonal trends in place and the possibility of a chase for performance in Q4, I am upgrading VIS to a buy. The valuation remains a risk.

VIS Industrials Closes at a New Record on Yom Kippur

According to the issuer, VIS "seeks to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of stocks in the Industrials sector." The ETF employs a "passively managed approach, using a full-replication strategy when possible and a sampling strategy if regulatory constraints dictate." The Industrials sector is broad and includes stocks of companies that "convert unfinished goods into finished durables used to manufacture other goods or provide services."

VIS is a medium-sized ETF with $6.6 billion in assets under management as of Oct. 1, 2025. That’s up from $5.6 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Shares are up 18%, dividends included, since then, underperforming the S&P 500 by about 2 percentage points. VIS sports a low annual expense ratio of just 9 basis points, while its trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 1.07% (about 0.2 percentage points below that of the S&P 500).

Share-price momentum has improved markedly from six months ago. Later, I’ll note some soft price trends on the technical view. For now, with a diversified portfolio but a somewhat elevated historical standard deviation, the ETF Risk Grade per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system is a lukewarm C. Finally, on liquidity, VIS looks good—average daily volume is 64,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is relatively tight at 5 basis points, per Vanguard.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. So, there is actually ample size and style diversification. Of course, VIS is a 100% U.S. allocation, and its price-to-earnings ratio remains concerning, above 24, and at a slight premium to the S&P 500’s earnings multiple.

Two areas that have recently been working within Industrials are Aerospace & Defense and AI-related names. Namely, the likes of GE Aerospace (GE), GE Vernova (GEV), and Caterpillar (CAT) have outperformed.

VIS: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Industrials is perhaps the most diverse of the 11 S&P 500 sectors. It’s also highly cyclical, so trends in U.S. economic growth play a leading role in how VIS performs.

Moreover, expectations around Fed policy can swing these blue chips. Hence, monitoring macro trends is key, and with the Q3 earnings season on the doorstep, we could see volatility kick up.

VIS: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally speaking, the October through November stretch has been exceptionally bullish when analyzing the past 10 years of price data.

I dug deeper into it and found that Industrials’ relative strength perks up starting on Oct. 29, according to data from Equity Clock. The next four weeks could be an opportune time to get long the fund if history is any guide.

VIS: Bullish October-November Trends

Sector Seasonal Strength Periods

The Technical Take

With a premium valuation and now at fresh all-time highs, VIS’s technical situation is good, but there are a couple of caution signs. Notice in the chart below that the RSI momentum oscillator at the top has printed a lower high despite the new high in price. That’s considered bearish divergence in technical analysis parlance. Furthermore, VIS has routinely tagged its rising 50-day moving average since mid-year. Thus, there could be a small dip this month that would be a more favorable entry than today.

But maybe being too cute is not the right approach. With a rising long-term 200dma and clear support down at $280, the risk/reward looks good. I also calculate a measured move upside price target of $335 based on the cup and handle formation annotated in blue in the chart below. A downside gap lingers at $260, but it would take a significant correction for that to get filled.

VIS: $335 Upside Target, Rising 200dma, Soft RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on VIS. Despite relative weakness since July, the chart appears favorable, and seasonal tailwinds should take hold. The mid-20s P/E ratio is a risk.

data.title: Omega Healthcare: The Recent Retracement Is An Opportunity
data.description:
Omega Healthcare Investors (NYSE:OHI) went through a difficult period after the pandemic as some of their critical operators faced significant challenges financially and operationally. There were some difficult quarters where the funds for distribution (FAD) weren’t enough to cover the dividend, and there was speculation that some operators would need to default on their obligations. The key word is speculation, because long-term investors who believed in OHI’s management team and read through the quarterly updates have been rewarded.

Since the April 2022 lows of around $25, shares of OHI have appreciated by 65.76% and delivered 14 dividends amounting to $9.38 in income. Shares of OHI broke out above $40 this summer and are trading near their all-time highs. A lot has changed since 2022. OHI is in a prime position to benefit from an aging population as they have over 1,000 skilled-nursing facilities and senior housing.

I am bullish on their triple net lease structure and the improving financials, as OHI raised their 2025 adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) guidance for 2025 on the Q2 conference call. I believe that the combination of improving financials, OHI’s operators getting stronger, and a lower-rate environment will push shares of OHI higher as 2025 comes to an end.

Following up on my previous article about OHI

I was previously bullish on OHI for a long time, but as the data changed at the end of 2024, I lowered my investment outlook to neutral. In March, I became bullish again as some of the headwinds I was worried about got corrected, and I upgraded my view to very bullish in my article, which was released in the middle of June (can be read here). Since June, shares of OHI have increased by 11.98%, which is the same as the S&P 500, and when the dividend is accounted for, OHI’s total return is 13.89%. I had discussed why I felt OHI is poised for long-term growth, as its success is fueled by an aging population in a protected sector with high barriers to entry. OHI’s triple-net lease structure, strategic acquisitions, and strong capital deployment positioned them for growth across their EBITDA and FFO. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I feel the recent retracement is an opportunity, as OHI raised guidance and is positioning themselves for future growth as they strengthen its portfolio.

OHI delivered strong Q2 earnings that has positioned them for future growth

OHI delivered $282.51 million in revenue, an increase of 11.8% YoY and exceeded expectations by $39.22 million. This is the most quarterly top-line revenue they have generated in years, which allowed them to produce strong amounts of profitability. OHI produced $213.5 million in FFO and $232 million in AFFO in Q2, which correlated to $0.77 per share of AFFO, which came in $0.03 ahead of expectations. The big statistic that I am focusing on is that OHI delivered $223 million in FAD, up from $177 million YoY. This took the FAD per share up from $0.68 to $0.74 YoY, which significantly exceeds the quarterly dividend of $0.67. OHI has been able to deliver on its critical success factors, which allowed them to increase their guidance in AFFO from $2.95-$3.01 to $3.04-$3.07 per share. The upward revision was attributed to the successful completion of $605 million in new investments, and a strategic financing decision to issue bonds instead of equity to address an upcoming debt maturity. OHI is issuing $600 million of 5.2% senior notes due in 2030 in a leverage-neutral transaction to repay notes maturing in 2026. OHI also issued 7 million common shares for gross proceeds of $258 million to prefund its investment pipeline and ended the quarter with $734 million in cash and a $1.45 billion credit facility that is largely untapped.

OHI completed $527 million in new investments during Q2, bringing its year-to-date total to $605 million. A significant portion of this activity was focused on their international operations as they finalized a $344 million acquisition of 45 care homes across the U.K., which will have a mix of new and existing operators. This transaction highlights that the U.K. is important to OHI's growth strategy, as it now accounts for approximately 65% of its new investment activity in 2025. OHI also invested $158 million in the U.S. to acquire 12 skilled nursing and assisted living facilities. These investments were structured with an attractive initial annual cash yield of 10% with built-in escalators. OHI also navigated some ongoing tenant issues as Genesis filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, which was outside the quarter. OHI feels that this is a necessary step to create a stronger operator, and they expect to receive their full contractual rent of $52 million annually during the bankruptcy proceedings. OHI’s leadership believes its 31-facility master lease with Genesis consists of highly desirable assets with strong coverage of 1.5x, which will make a lease rejection unlikely during the proceedings. One of their other major tenants, LaVie, successfully emerged from bankruptcy on June 1, with the master lease being assumed by a new entity, Avardis. OHI’s lease terms are intact, and Avardis is making full contractual payments.

I believe that OHI is strategically positioned for continued growth as they have over $2 billion in liquidity, providing them with the firepower to fund new investments that will be accretive to earnings. On the earnings call, management discussed that they have an active acquisition pipeline for the second half of 2025, as there is an increased number of opportunities in the U.S. and the U.K. OHI has their sights set on regional senior housing portfolios available at prices below replacement cost and numerous skilled nursing facilities. OHI's investment strategy focuses on structuring deals that are immediately accretive as they target initial cash yields of 10%. OHI plans to continue leveraging its relationships with its 92 existing operators, which have sourced 70% of its investments over the last five years, as their corporate development team cultivates new operator partnerships. The team is also looking at alternative lease structures outside their triple net leases, such as RIDEA-like arrangements in senior housing, which would allow OHI to better align with operators who are less inclined towards traditional triple-net leases and to potentially capture more upside from operational improvements. I believe that taking a multifaceted growth strategy outside the triple net lease structure is beneficial and could add many new deals as we enter a multi-decade increase in demand for the facilities in OHI’s portfolio.

OHI Still Looks Like It Is Trading At An Attractive Valuation

When I compare OHI to Sabra Health Care (SBRA), National Health Investors (NHI), Physicians Realty Trust (DOC), and Healthcare Realty Trust (HR) it still seems like it's trading at an attractive valuation to me. OHI trades at a price to FFO of 13.92x, slightly higher than the peer group average of 12.88x but well within a value range. OHI’s net debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 4.25x when the peer group average is 5.37x. This means that OHI hasn’t overextended itself to solidify new deals.

REITs have traditionally been income investments for me, so I want to make sure I am exceeding the risk-free rate of return through their dividend yield. OHI has a dividend yield of 6.44%, above the peer group average of 5.87% and well above the risk-free rate of return that is less than 4% on shorter-term bonds. OHI’s FFO coverage ratio is also at 111.57%, as the dividend is now fully covered by its FFO. This is a large improvement from previous quarters, where the dividend payments weren’t fully covered. This gives me a lot of confidence that the dividend will remain intact.

Risks to investing in OHI

While I am still bullish on OHI, there are several risks to consider. Investors should understand that OHI is a REIT that specializes in leasing skilled nursing or senior living facilities to external operators. If the underlying operators fail to maximize their revenue or have operational hardships, then there could be issues collecting rent in the future. OHI’s revenue depends on stability from Medicare and Medicaid reimbursements, as their operators could be negatively impacted by regulatory changes, which could potentially lower reimbursement rates. In Q2, OHI started looking at new lease structures that would open them up to additional deals, but it would strip away some protections that the triple net structure provides them, which could negatively impact them in the future if they are forced to allocate additional capital toward their properties that they wouldn’t have had to do under the triple net structure. This could create a situation where margins contract.

Conclusion

I am still bullish on OHI over $40 per share, as they have 94% of their rent and interest tied to triple net leases, with 91% of their rent and interest tied to fixed-rate escalators. OHI is generating a 2.3% average annualized increase in its revenue from these contracts. Through the triple net lease structure, OHI can protect its interest as the responsibility for taxes and repairs are the tenants responsibility. I’m pleased with management's operational execution, as they have guided the company through a dark period, and now the FAD is exceeding the dividend payments, and OHI raised their AFFO guidance for 2025. OHI continues to manage its portfolio of assets to generate value for shareholders while paying a large dividend that continues to outpace the risk-free rate of return. I think that shares of OHI will continue to generate capital appreciation and strong income for shareholders as we get the Q3 numbers and finish out the 2025 fiscal year.

data.title: Orla Mining: Musselwhite Acquisition Doubles Output, With Gold Price Tailwind
data.description:
Introduction

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has climbed out it's recent trading range to a new record above $3,800 an ounce, up more than 40% this year after a 26% rise in 2024. For gold miners, whose earnings are dependent directly on the gold price, this has been a boom time. The VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) has more than doubled this year, with the latest rally sending it even higher.

Orla Mining Ltd. (NYSE:ORLA) has taken part in this rally, nearly doubling in 2025. Yet despite the gold price surge, the stock still trades below its value in April, lagging peers. A pit wall failure at its Camino Roja mine led to a suspension in mining, and a small 5% cut to guidance. The market seems to have penalized this incident disproportionately.

With gold at record highs and operations stable, Orla shares should be well positioned to restart their rally. In this article, I want to explore Orla’s most recent results, examine the Camino Mine pit wall collapse in more detail, and explain why I believe Orla is a buy at today’s price.

Q2 Earnings

Orla Mining reported their Q2 ’25 results at the start of August, showing a strong increase in production. Gold production reached 77,811 ounces, more than double last year’s 33,206 ounces for the same period.

This growth resulted from the newly acquired Musselwhite Mine in Ontario, which added 52,666 ounces at a grade of 5.52 g/t. Conversely, production at Orla’s other mine, the Camino Roja mine in Mexico, came in at 25,145 ounces, a 24% decline year over year. The decline was driven by a 40% reduction in ore stacked on the heap leach pad and a decline in grades (0.57 g/t vs. 0.66 g/t). Importantly, this decline was not unexpected and was in line with the mine plan and not due to any operational set back (the pit wall collapse having occurred in the third quarter).

Orla achieved an average realized gold price of $3,251 an ounce during the quarter, 39% higher than the previous year, but below that of peers. This discount to what peers achieved reflects a gold pre-pay agreement which was required for funding of the Musselwhite mine acquisition. This $360 million gold pre-pay, based on a $2,834 an ounce gold price, requires the delivery of 4,025 ounces of gold per month for the period March 2025 to February 2028.

The combination of a higher gold price and increased production, led to revenues of $263.7 million, over triple the same period last year. With the recent rally in the gold price since quarter end, Q3 could be even better.

Orla generated cash flow from operations of $102.7 million during the quarter which translated into $64.2 million of free cash flow. Orla ended the quarter with $215.4 million in cash against $ 420 million of debt, for a net debt to EBITDA ratio of just 0.63, indicating a strong balance sheet.

However, this is a marked change from the previous year's $160.8 million net cash position. At a time when many other miners are switching from net debt to net cash, Orla has moved the other way. The reason for this is their $810 million acquisition of the Musselwhite mine, funded through a combination of existing cash holdings, undrawn debt facilities, new convertible debt, and a gold pre-pay.

Given the grand scale of the purchase relative to Orla’s size, it’s striking the company is already on track to return to a net cash position by the end of 2027. This is of course partly down to the record gold price, which has given Orla a financial boost post-acquisition.

Moving onto cost and margins, Orla reported all-in sustaining costs [AISC] of $1,421 per ounce, an increase of $639. Whilst this represents a significant increase, when we dig into the details a bit more, we can see this is largely due to the recent acquisition as opposed to underlying weakness at the Camino Rojo mine.

At Camino Rojo AISC came in at $690 per ounce, a decrease of $92 from the previous year. Pushing up the overall AISC was the recently acquired, higher cost Musselwhite mine with an AISC of $1,663 per ounce.

Overall, this translated to a net income of $48.2 million, a $23.9 million improvement from a year earlier. On an adjusted basis, earnings also saw a rise from $23.0 million to $64.2 million, equivalent to a $0.20 earnings per share.

This was a strong quarter for Orla. Production saw a large increase following its acquisition of the Musselwhite mine, and with the continuing rise in the gold price, this acquisition keeps looking even better. Whilst Camino Rojo saw a fall in production, this was expected, and AISC fell. With precious metals having rallied since quarter end, Q3 looks set up to be an even stronger quarter.

Mines Overview & Growth Outlook

Musselwhite Mine

Musselwhite now stands as Orla’s flagship asset. An underground mine in Ontario, Canada, a tier one mining jurisdiction, annual production is around 200,000 ounces with a current mine life reaching until 2030. The mine has an AISC currently around $1,400 per ounce.

Whilst this is a recent acquisition for Orla, the mine itself has been operating since 1997, producing over 6 million ounces of gold. Orla acquired Musselwhite from Newmont (NEM) for $810 million as part of Newmont’s plan to divest non-core assets.

This acquisition was funded through a combination of existing cash holdings, undrawn debt facilities, new convertible debt, and a gold pre-pay. With 1.5 million ounces of reserves with a grade of 6.23 g/t, this is a purchase price of $540 per ounce, not a bad price when you compare to the current price of gold.

There is strong exploration potential for this asset. Current drilling shows reserves at depth, with drilling showing potential extensions more than 1km beyond current reserves. An exploration program is currently underway to expand resources and extend mine life beyond 2030.

Camino Rojo Mine

Orla’s Camino Rojo mine in Zacatecas, Mexico, is its other operational asset. It is an open pit heap leach mine, with an AISC averaging around $900, placing it amongst the lowest quartile for gold mining costs globally. Annual production sits at approximately 100,000 ounces.

Like Musselwhite, there is plenty of exploration potential at this asset. Beyond the existing oxides, the underground sulphide deposit represents the longer term focus for the mine. With 4.2 Moz in measured and indicated resources and high-grade intercepts continuing to extend Zone 22, Camino Rojo has the potential to be developed into a much larger underground mine. A PEA is expected in 2026, and early drilling results—such as 9.8 g/t AuEq over 9.4 m show the underground mines potential.

One of most recent developments for this mine was in July, where the mine experienced a pit wall collapse. Luckily, there were no injuries and minimal equipment damage, or environmental impacts, and the pit could still be accessed. After a geotechnical review, it was decided that a stabilization plan would be implemented, pushing the north wall where the movement occurred back 50-80 meters. 9 million tonnes of material from this stabilization plan at a 0.74 g/t grade will be added to the heap leach pad, meaning no ounces will be lost.

This incident has led to slightly lowered guidance for Camino Rojo in 2025, with production set to be 95-105 thousand ounces from 110-120 thousand ounces, with AISC modestly higher at $800-$900/oz. While this is a near-term setback, the issue has been contained with a solution identified ensuring any output reduction should largely be deferred rather than lost.

South Railroad Project

Alongside its producing mine, Orla owns the South Railroad project in Nevada within one of the most prolific gold belts. The feasibility study outlined a open-pit heap leach mine with average production of 124,000 ounces per year over an initial 8 year mine life. This is expected to be a lower cost mine with a forecast AISC of just $1,021 per ounce over the mine’s lifetime.

Exploration upside is significant. South Railroad sits on a 25,000 hectare land package with multiple targets.

A Record of Decision is expected by 2026 giving final permits for construction. This would enable production by 2028. Combined with Orla’s other two mines and their planned expansions, this could result in annual production of over 500,000 ounces in 2029.

Valuation

With all miner's value closely tied to the underlying price of gold, the first way I like to determine an initial value for a miner is against its peers. For this, I use an enterprise to EBITDA multiple, a metric that captures both earnings but also the balance sheet, which varies between miners.

I opted to compare Orla Mining to peers Alamos Gold (AGI), Kinross Gold (KGC), New Gold (NGD), Equinox Gold (EQX), AngloGold Ashanti (AU), B2GOLD (BTG), SSRM Mining (SSRM), and Newmont (NEM).

Orla Mining currently trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio of 7.488. This places it almost exactly at the average value of its peers. It trades above peers Anglogold Ashanti, and B2Gold, justified given their exposure to weaker jurisdictions such as Mali, and other non-tier one jurisdictions. On the other side it trades below the likes of Equinox and Alamos, much larger producers, and with operations primarily located in Canada. I believe on this metric Orla Mining appear to be fairly valued, though one could argue that with the majority of production from Canada and greater growth prospects than some other names, a slight premium could be warranted.

To determine a price value for Orla Mining, I calculate the estimated cash flow from operations for 2026 and apply a multiple to this. Musselwhite is set to planned to produce 240,000 ounces based on the life of mine plan, with Camino Rojo set to produce 114,000 ounces for total production in 2026 of 351,000 ounces.

I use the midpoint of AISC guidance for 2025, $1450, with a slight adjustment lower due to higher production and an adjustment slightly higher to account for the impact of royalty payments at higher gold prices. I assume a 30% tax rate and interest costs of zero, given Orla Mining is set to be in a net-cash position by the end of next year.

Up until recently I have been using a $3,300 an ounce gold price assumption, but given the continuing strong performance of gold, I have decided to now up this to $3,600 an ounce. This is still below the current gold price, giving some margin of safety.

I anticipate a cash flow from operations of $1.62 per share in 2026 and apply a cash flow multiplier of x8, the multiple I believe an average miner should trade at. With operations primarily coming from a tier one jurisdiction, with multiple growth prospects, at a just below sector average AISC, there is an argument a higher multiple could be used, but I don’t want to be too over optimistic, Orla does still have only 2 operational mines. Applying an 8x multiplier on $1.62 per share of cash flow gives a target price of $12.96, 25% higher than the current share price.

Based on these calculations and looking at Orla’s operations, I believe Orla’s shares deserve a buy rating at this price. They have a clear plan in place to grow production with 150,000 ounces per year from South Railroad, a potential Camino Rojo underground, and further exploration potential at their current assets. Combined the gold price continuing its upward trend, and the balance sheet likely to return to a net cash position next year, Orla looks like its in a strong position. With this in mind, I assign a Buy rating to Orla Mining.

As for risks, the primary risk is the same for any gold miner: the price of gold. Any significant reduction in the price of gold will significantly reduce earnings and risk sending the share price lower. And it goes without saying that operational risks coming to be would have an incredibly negative impact on the share price; the recent pit wall collapse at Camino Rojo is certainly concerning, but one only has to look at the SSR Mining’s Çöpler mine or Victoria Gold’s Eagle Gold Mine heap leach pad collapse to see the how it could have been even worse, the latter pushing Victoria Gold into receivership.

Conclusion

Orla Mining has managed to double production through the acquisition of Musselwhite and diversify into a tier one jurisdiction. With a record gold price Orla is generating strong levels of cash flow, with it on track to return to a net cash position by the end of 2027.

Whilst the pit wall collapse at Camino Rojo has resulted in a small cut in guidance this year, this appears to be minor, and Camino Rojo is otherwise performing strongly. With several near-term growth prospects, and shares on my calculations being undervalued, I assign Orla Mining a Buy rating.

data.title: Buying Treasuries: Keep It Short, Or Lock In Today's Long Rates?
data.description:
Things have changed a lot since the last time I wrote about fixed income strategies when there were lots of completely safe fixed income offerings with yields of 5 to 6%. The Fed has begun to cautiously lower rates. Unemployment numbers are rising. And the market as a whole seems convinced that we are going to return to an era of lower than historically average yields similar to what prevailed from 2008-2021.

Those of us who prefer to use treasury bonds rather than dividend stocks for income face a challenging decision. What maturities should we invest in to produce predictable income that keeps up with inflation with the least possible risk?

In this article I'll summarize both the arguments for locking in current rates with longer duration bonds and the reasons why you might do the very opposite and keep your fixed income investments very, very short.

The Argument for Investing in Long Maturity Bonds

To understand the dilemma bond investors face now, you need to be aware of how the Federal Reserve's Open Market Committee attempts to control bond rates. As stated on the Federal Reserve Board's website, they do this by setting "a target range for the federal funds rate (the rate at which depository institutions lend to each other)."

This rate applies to very short term loans--overnight loans, in fact. But though they only set this very short-term rate, changes in the Fed rate rate directly influence the rates of bonds of all maturities. Typically the Fed raises its rate when the economy is running hot and lowers it, often dramatically, during recessionary periods, At least that is what they've done during the last 25 years. That's the amount of investing experience of a large proportion of those investing now.

Key to understanding the dilemma is this: rates over those past 25 years have been, for the most part, extremely low.

Here is the Federal Funds rate as set by the FOMC from 2000 to 2025

As you can see, the Federal Funds rate over a quarter of a century has very rarely reached higher than 5% and has spent much of it's time well under 1%. That makes today's Federal Funds rate of 4.22% an outlier. This has encouraged investors to lock in today's rates by buying Treasury bonds with fairly long durations, some of which extending as far out as 30 years. In return, they ask for no risk premium.

The current 30 year rate as of October 2, 2025 is 4.705%. The one month rate as I write is 4.11% Clearly investors who buy these bonds believe that rates will drop again, especially as the job market appears to be cooling and that they will be very happy to be receiving 4.705% when rates are again hovering between 1 and 2%.

The popularity of these long duration bonds is bolstered by the fact that many investors buy their bonds via mutual funds and ETFs. Popular bond ETFs like Vanguard Total Bond Market (BND) hold a lot of longer duration treasuries, so that the fund as a whole has an average effective maturity of 8.1 years. A full 19.63% of its holdings will mature over a period stretching from 10 years to over 25 years. (Based on Vanguard's most recently published data.)

But when we broaden our time perspective by a few decades we see a far different pattern.

Treasury Rates Were Very Different Before 2000

As you can see, from 1955 through 2000, the average Federal Fund rate was far higher than what we have become accustomed to in the past quarter century. Rates surged to 8.90% in 1970. When the Fed lowered its rate to what seemed like a refreshingly low 3.51% in January of 1972 to combat a recession (gray columns on the graph) they couldn't hold it there.

As soon as the economy began to recover, the Federal Funds rate surged. It ended at 12.92% in July of 1974, even though the economy was again in recession. When Fed once again attempted to lower rates, and drop their rate to 4.61% in January of 1977, again it couldn't hold them there. By June of 1981 the Federal Funds rate was a whopping 19.10%!

Inflation Made the Difference

The reason for the very high rates that prevailed earlier in US economic history was that the nation underwent a period of high inflation. You can see this on the graph below.

Prices increased relentlessly throughout the decades from 1965 to 1981. Annual inflation from 1975 to 1981 looked like this:

Year Annual Inflation Rate (%)
1965 1.2
1966 2.4
1967 3.6
1968 4.6
1969 5.8
1970 6.3
1971 4.7
1972 3.0
1973 3.6
1974 8.3
1975 9.1
1976 6.5
1977 6.3
1978 7.4
1979 9.8
1980 12.4
1981 10.4

This annual inflation compounded to the point where, in 1981 it took $2.89 to buy something that had been $1.00 in 1965. That represents an overall compounded inflation rate of 188.6%!

You can imagine what would have happened to an investor who bought a 10 year treasury in 1971, at a time when the market was pricing its yield at 6.14%. By the time that bond matured, the buying power of that $1000 bond's principal would have decreased to $445.54! Even with the interest the investor received, ignoring the impact of taxes, their real wealth would have taken a serious hit had it been invested in long bonds over this period.

Investors have been very confident that this kind of runaway inflation can't happen again. The ability of the Federal Reserve to tame inflation through the imposition of extremely high rates in the early 1980s has convinced most investors of this. Based on the FOMC's latest report from their September meeting, the Federal Reserve still believes that though Trumps tariffs will be inflationary, the impact will be short lived. A majority believe that inflation, and hence the Fed's rate can be expected to decline over the next three years to no more than 3% a year. If you believe this, you can be comfortable tying up your money in 10 year bonds as their current yield at 4.09% is still considerably above 3%.

The Future of Inflation Is More Cloudy Than it Has been for Decades

Unfortunately, there are quite a few reasons to be less confident that inflation will be tamed. I'm old enough to have been an adult in 1980 and am particularly sensitive to the way that the inflation of the past three years is reminding me of the climate of the middle 70s.

Here are the issues that may fuel another bout of significant, compounding inflation over the next decade:

1. The psychological impact of the last 3 years of inflation. Inflation is all about what people think will happen, not just actual economic developments. When consumers see grocery prices that are 30% higher than they were just a few years ago and hear that Trump's tariffs may raise them another 15%, they begin to expect inflation to rise that dramatically.

Almost every month now I notice that the prices of items I buy at discount grocers are rising by 5% or more even at discount grocers. The impact of this cumulative inflation is that even if inflation is reported to be "only" 3%, middle class consumers feel like inflation is really 30% and are cutting back on discretionary purchases. This has been reported by retailers like Walmart (WMT) in recent earnings calls.

The restaurant business is struggling as patrons think twice about paying $25 for a hamburger and fries and a drink at their local pub. Fast food restaurants price has made it so that it is no longer cheap to take the whole family out for dinner when you don't feel like cooking. And even if you cook that burger at home, the price of hamburger has soared. We are paying almost $7/lb for 80/20 at our local market. This doesn't feel to the consumer who drives our economy like 3% inflation. It feels like 50% inflation!

2. The Trump administrations' erratic, unpredictable stance on all economic policy. Companies, investors, and consumers have been getting different messages every month, week and sometimes even day about what tariffs the president will impose on the imports that represent much of the food, clothing, and hard goods consumers buy. Not only that, but the Supreme Court has yet to rule on whether he even had the ability to impose those tariffs adds even more uncertainty.

3. Companies fearing future inflation will raise their prices now. Just as consumers change their behavior fearing inflation, so do companies. With the uncertainty that the administration has introduced into rates, raw material prices, and the viability of international sales, companies will respond defensively by raising prices now to cover the cost of the products they plan to sell next year because they can't predict those product's raw costs.

4. The threat to the dollar's supremacy. The isolationism that has become fashionable among the president's supporters and the dramatic shift in the tone of negotiation and collaboration with leaders of our erstwhile allies has alienated these leaders and the citizens in countries around the world. There is increasing talk among the leaders of increasingly wealthy ex-U.S. nations like China, India, and Brazil that have large, growing economies about going off the dollar as the world's reserve currency. If the dollar loses that status, the U.S. government can no longer pay its debts by printing money and manipulating rates.

5. Bond Vigilantes. If Trump gets his wish and fills the Federal Reserve Board with puppets and continues on its path weakening the power of the Bureau of Labor Statistics and other Federal agencies to collect economic data that can be trusted, bond buyers will take things into their own hands. Right now, most big investors still believe the statistics we have been given and make decisions based on that data. If that changes and they lose confidence they can trust the U.S. government's data, and perhaps even its future ability to honor its debt, they will take a defensive posture. That means they will demand a far higher risk premium before they lend money to the United States by buying their Treasury bonds. Or they may exit that market, which would cause rates to skyrocket.

If Inflation Continues to Rise The Shortest Maturities Are the Safest

Back in 1980, when I first had some spare money to invest, the T. Rowe Price Prime Reserve Money Market Fund was yielding 17% a year. That appeared to be the best way to keep up with inflation. We had no way of knowing that inflation would be controlled by the imposition of these high rates. All we knew was that every week the price of a can of soup was going up by 2 or 3%. When inflation finally tamed our money was there, ready to invest in a market that was at last waking from a long period of the doldrums, which resulted in the famous 1979 "Death of Equities" Businessweek article.

Even investors who didn't invest in stocks, continued to have fine money market rates that over the decade of the 1990s kept up with inflation and outperformed many stock investments. I was getting 7% in money market funds throughout the 90s when inflation was far tamer. I was also getting similar yields from long term bond funds, whose NAV rose as rates slowly declined through the next decade.

Bottom Line

Right now a ladder of very short term treasury rates, with maturities from one to four months or a treasury money market fund is an excellent option for investors who are not comfortable with the risks I just outlined above. The good thing about investing your fixed income in instruments with short maturities is that they give you flexibility and protect you against a dramatic loss of buying power.

If we have a recession and the Fed cuts its rate, stock prices may respond as they usually do in prolonged recessions and plummet to where they are a far better value than they are now. If this happens, investors who have short bond ladders will have money maturing every month with which to invest in the market. If rates do drop, they can sell Treasury bills get back more than they paid to buy stocks with.

If inflation persists, and consumer and corporate psychology becomes anchored on inflationary expectations that in themselves drive inflation higher, you will be able to reinvest your fixed income as it matures in new short term issues whose yields will rise with inflation.

And should the destabilization of international markets we are seeing now reach critical mass, you can stop buying treasuries at all and invest your money in fixed income offered in more stable currencies.

data.title: The Sora 2 Shock: Adobe's Moat Is Eroding
data.description:
The launch of Sora 2 from OpenAI is the strongest indicator to date that the creative software sector's gravitational center point has shifted to generative software. Sora 2 does not merely reproduce or “generate videos." Rather, it generates motion that has physically plausible constraints, it keeps certain states of the world consistent across takes, and it also generates simultaneous speech and sound effects - all based off of a prompt. It is an elevation, in the developer's references, in fidelity, controllability, and audio-visual fidelity, and it is available immediately via a new Sora app for iOS, with wider availability later.

OpenAI has attached the model to an app for social creation, rather than classic post-production. The invitational Sora app rolls out in the U.S. & Canada, allows people to remix trends, and includes “cameos,” allowing users to opt-in for friends to drop their likeness and voice into generated scenes, with controls for co-ownership and removal. The app is limited to 10-second generations for now, but it’s clear where it’s going.

The Quality Curve Has Bent Upward

If you’ve spent the past year indulging in user-created internet videos, you will have already figured out how rapidly these systems have advanced. Now some intuition has some slight formalization with receipts. Sora 2’s research formalization and disclosures reflect not just prettier frames but better physics that old systems broke regularly: balls bounce not teleport, rigid bodies interact with other rigid bodies, and buoyant things drift like buoyant things. It all sounds wild… until you realize that it is literally that “world modeling” which separates toy demos from video footage that you could reasonably assign a campaign or a story to. The motion of human experience from slick to useable is happening in quarters, not decades. OpenAI also is not alone. The field has undergone a release train roll-out. Google’s Veo 3 is now providing text-to-video on consumer surfaces and new pro tools.

Adobe’s Best Case Now Looks Like “Wrapper”

Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) is awake to this new reality. Firefly Video left limited access and went to public beta in February; it creates five (5) second 1080p clips with text-to-video or image-to-video and introduces new translation capabilities. That was a nice place for them to stake a claim, but it immediately appeared limited against the ambition and visual fidelity of the newest strong wave of video models. And Adobe documentation shows where this is going: Firefly’s web app allows you to select non-Adobe engines - Veo 3, Luma, Runway, Topaz - and Adobe is positioning its product as if you will ultimately “select” best-of-breed models, not that the best model is housed there. That’s a high-quality wrapper. Wrappers can be good; they usually don’t get you software multiples.

Adobe’s price restructuring underscores the wrapper framework. Plans for Creative Cloud are restructuring to a Standard/Pro split with metered “generative credits” for pro features, including video. The plans' pages also highlight their “choice to use non-Adobe generative AI models” such as OpenAI’s GPT and Google’s Imagen and Veo, as well as Flux and others. Practical, but also reveals that Adobe’s moat isn’t about model performance anymore - it’s billing, bundling and UI across a federated set of engines. If the furthest frontier of model is being set by others, Adobe’s place is orchestration - not inception.

Distribution Is Product Now

The Sora application does not act solely as a demo launcher, it is a creative feed. This is important because it eliminates the old pipeline - capture in Device A, edit in Software B, export, and finally publish to Platform C. When world-class generation and social distribution overlap in time, the tool that controls the “save as .mp4” stage is supplanted by the tool that owns the network where people’s ideas distribute. Adobe has the world-class editors. However, OpenAI is trying to own the territory where cultural trends are born and evolve. As we cycle forward, “where things are created” will evolve to become “where they are observed,” and that’s not Adobe.

In the meantime, the rest of the ecosystem continues to get stickier. YouTube and Shorts are employing generative video experimentation, while social-consumer surface integrations and marketing stack integration points for Veo push creation onto the platforms audiences are already using. Every shot created inside of a distribution surface is one less shot to use a separate timeline editor for (and each one teaches the model to build a better one).

Metered Creativity Meets Free-to-Try Generators

Adobe's usage-metered approach for the generative features of Firefly (monthly renewed credits, increasing “cost” incurred on longer/advanced outputs, monthly add-on plans to buy more) are all rational from the standpoint of compute economics, but provide some nasty product friction when your rival rolls out a headline model that ships with "generous" no-cost free limits at the app layer, and a Pro tier available to already paying subscribers of a larger AI service. Early adopters of Firefly are already airing grievances to users in community forums due to low beta ceilings on video generations, combined with the feeling of paying double: once for the Creative Cloud subscription, and once for meaningful creative executions. When scaled to mass use, what is friction for policy, is friction for the product, and friction for the product is churn.

The Financial Setup Doesn’t Pay for Wrapper-Level Returns

Adobe is still a wonderful company; however, valuation is about future cash flows in a new regime. The company grew its record Q3 FY25 revenue of 11% YoY to $5.99 billion. Adobe has a market cap of roughly $150–151 billion forward multiple in the mid-teens. Those are not nose bleed numbers, but they are software-like multiples that assume a durable growth and moat – both of which may not be guaranteed if the best content increasingly comes from generative systems that Adobe doesn’t control. If AI video becomes a model-selection problem rather than editor-selection problem, the wrapper’s margin and growth profile will tend to be compressed toward the lowest cost and highest quality model available at a point in time. That’s not something you pay 6–7x sales on.

The Strategic Dead-End: You Can’t Out-Run the Frontier You Don’t Own

The challenge of being a wrapper is you are simultaneously squeezed between two forces you don’t control: model vendors above you and end users below you. When model quality experiences a step-function improvement, you will be out of differentiation unless you provide a way to jump with it. Jumping with it means either developing a frontier-class research effort, which is incredibly expensive and computationally intensive, or paying for those advancements from others (which renders your product a switchboard). Adobe’s recent efforts, including explicitly allowing non-Adobe engines in Firefly, and having credits for the generative model from partners, are smart short-term product decisions that read as long-term strategy compromises. You try those things when you know that the best model is not likely going to be yours.

Why Sora 2 Is the Inflection Point

Sora 2 lands perfectly at the intersection of quality, control, audio, and distribution – these are the four vectors that have historically made Adobe relevant. Their videos not only look better; they function better as well. It can reproduce multi-shot direction and maintain scene continuity, and align speech and soundscapes appropriately with what you see. Then, they offer a composite all of those functionality in a network that builds incentives for creation and remixing.

The counterargument is well known: Adobe's brand, file formats, and enterprise penetration are unmatched. All of that is true. But brand and file formats are only moats if teams still have to use those formats. Unfortunately for Adobe, these new GenAI video models may make traditional formats completely obsolete.

The Bearish Bottom Line

Adobe is transitioning from inventor to integrator precisely as video generation transitions from gimmick to production. Sora 2 is the instant where the strategic risk becomes hard for people to overlook: a model that simulates reality well enough to allow for a mainstream application, with an app that captures generation into social loops. Adobe’s reaction of giving credits, creating bundles, and choosing 3rd party engines, still does not solve for the fundamental issue of the true intelligence living elsewhere. That can be a solid business. It’s just not the type of irreplaceable platform that warrants a rich, compounding multiple against rising competitive speed and declining ARR growth.

At best, Adobe is the shiny facade serving a collection of engines it doesn’t actually own. At worst, it becomes the endpoint out of which you export after all the interesting work is done. Either way, Sora 2 advances the timeline on both scenarios. For a company still valued like a category king, that’s an issue.
